title: Policy Rules and Targets: Framing the Central Banker's Problem

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: Stephen Cecchetti, Director of Research at the New York Fed, argues in this June 1998 Economic Policy Review article that central banks adopting rigid inflation targets risk creating significant output growth variability. Using 1984-95 data, he demonstrates that the output-inflation variability trade-off is extremely steep, meaning modest decreases in inflation variability cause large deviations in GDP growth from its optimal path. The paper recommends that inflation-targeting central banks adopt range targets rather than point targets to retain flexibility for output stabilization.
key_points: Central banks with rigid inflation targets may experience sharp rises in GDP growth volatility while attempting to control price fluctuations.
Analysis using 1984-95 data shows the output-inflation variability trade-off is extremely steep.
Point-target inflation regimes force central banks to increase weight on inflation relative to output variability, which may have undesirable side effects.
The Reserve Bank of Australia targets 2-3 percent inflation averaged over the business cycle, exemplifying a flexible range-based approach.
New Zealand and UK central banks also announced target ranges rather than point targets for inflation.
Cecchetti concludes that policymakers should ask whether they truly care so little about output and real quantities when considering price-only based policies.
why_it_matters: This 1998 research provides theoretical foundations for understanding why pure inflation targeting may be suboptimal. It remains relevant to historical debates on monetary policy frameworks, supporting the case for flexible inflation targets or dual mandates over rigid price-level targeting.
macro_context: The paper addresses a fundamental tension in monetary policy: the impossibility of simultaneously stabilizing both output and prices at their optimal levels. Central banks must balance the costs of output fluctuations against the costs of price fluctuations, and rigid inflation targeting implicitly shifts weight toward price stability at the expense of output stability.
important_numbers: 2-3 percent (Reserve Bank of Australia inflation target range over business cycle)
